required.title: [Read Aloud] African creation stories and Orisha tales

suggested.title: [Look Together] West African art, Adinkra symbols, and Nigerian festival images

competence_markers.description: The student can describe the Yoruba understanding of God and the orishas, including at least three orishas by name and domain
The student understands ancestor veneration as a serious belief — that the dead remain present and active in the community
The student can explain ubuntu in their own words and discuss what it means for how people relate to each other
The student can narrate how African religious traditions survived the Middle Passage through creative adaptation
The student treats African traditions with the same seriousness and respect given to any other religion in 1.3
The student engages with narration prompts with specific detail and personal reflection

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Yoruba idea of God.
narration_prompts.detail: Who is Olodumare? Why do people not pray to him directly? What are the orishas?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick an orisha and tell me their story.
narration_prompts.detail: What part of life do they govern? What are they like as a person? Why would someone honor them?
What does "I am because we are" mean? How is this different from the idea that each person is a completely separate individual?
Why do many African peoples believe the dead are still present? How does this change the way families work?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of how African traditions survived the slave trade.
narration_prompts.detail: How did people keep their beliefs alive? What did they do when they were forbidden to worship?
What kind of character is he? Why do so many cultures have trickster stories?

cross_connections.description: African civilizations are among the world's oldest. The student connects religious traditions to the civilizations that produced them.
The Yoruba and Bantu worldviews answer the same deep questions every tradition addresses — but their answers are distinctive.
The comparison between Olodumare/orishas and the Christian God/saints is one of the most productive comparisons in Stretching Branches.
Both Norse and Yoruba traditions include trickster figures (Loki, Eshu). Both Celtic and Yoruba traditions treat the natural world as sacred.
Ancestor veneration, sacred landscape, and oral tradition appear across African, Aboriginal, and Native American traditions.

content.why_this_matters:
Africa is the oldest inhabited continent on Earth. Human beings have lived there longer than anywhere else. And for as long as people have lived in Africa, they have had beliefs about God, about the spirits in the world around them, about the ancestors who came before, and about how human beings should live together.

These beliefs are not museum pieces. They are alive right now, practiced by millions of people across Africa and around the world. Some of these traditions survived one of the worst things that ever happened in human history — the Atlantic slave trade — and emerged on the other side changed but unbroken. That survival is one of the most remarkable stories in all of religion.

In this packet, you will learn about people who believe that God is so vast and absolute that you cannot approach God directly — you need divine helpers, called orishas or spirits, who carry your prayers and guide your life. You will learn about people who believe the dead are not gone — that your grandmother, your great-grandfather, and all who came before you are still present, still watching, still part of the family. And you will learn about a powerful idea called ubuntu, which says that no person is fully a person alone — that you become who you are through your relationships with other people.

These are not primitive ideas. They are some of the deepest and most important ideas human beings have ever had.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness that Africa has sophisticated religious traditions** — not "animism" or "superstition" but complete systems of belief about God, human nature, and right living
2. **The concept of orishas as divine forces** — the Yoruba understanding that God's power is expressed through specific forces in nature and human life
3. **Ancestor veneration** — the belief that the dead remain part of the living community
4. **Ubuntu as a way of understanding what it means to be human** — "I am because we are"
5. **The sacred quality of the natural world** — rivers, forests, and places as carriers of divine presence
6. **Respect and seriousness** — these traditions deserve the same attention given to any religion in 1.3
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Africa — The Oldest Home of the Oldest Beliefs

Human beings have lived in Africa longer than anywhere else on Earth. That means the earliest beliefs, the earliest stories about God and the world, were African. For tens of thousands of years, African peoples developed ways of understanding the universe that are among the most ancient and most sophisticated on the planet.

Africa is also enormous — the second-largest continent, with over fifty countries and thousands of different peoples, each with their own language and their own traditions. When we talk about "African traditional religions," we are not talking about one religion. We are talking about hundreds of traditions, each with its own character. In this packet, we will focus on a few of the most important ones — especially the Yoruba tradition of West Africa and the Bantu traditions of East and Southern Africa — while always remembering that these are just part of a much larger picture.

### Part 2: Olodumare — The God Who Is Too Great to Approach

The Yoruba people of what is now Nigeria believe in one supreme God: Olodumare (also called Olorun). Olodumare created everything that exists. He is all-powerful and all-knowing.

But here is something interesting: the Yoruba do not worship Olodumare directly. He is too vast, too absolute, too far beyond human understanding to approach with ordinary prayers and offerings. Instead, Olodumare's power flows through divine beings called orishas, who are closer to human life and who can be approached, honored, and asked for help.

This might remind you of something: in many Christian traditions, people pray to saints as intermediaries who carry their prayers to God. The Yoruba orisha system works in a similar way — a supreme God whose power reaches humans through specific divine helpers, each responsible for a different part of life.

### Part 3: The Orishas — Divine Forces in the World

Each orisha governs a specific part of life and nature. They are not distant, abstract gods. They have personalities, preferences, and stories. Learning about them is like learning about powerful, complicated people who care deeply about the world.

**Eshu (also called Elegba):** The orisha of the crossroads — of choices, of messages, of the space between one thing and another. Every ritual begins by honoring Eshu first, because he is the one who opens the path between humans and all the other orishas. He is a trickster — playful, wise, and sometimes dangerous. Without Eshu, nothing moves.

**Ogun:** The orisha of iron, work, and justice. He is the god of anyone who works with metal — blacksmiths, mechanics, surgeons. In Nigeria today, witnesses in some courtrooms swear their oaths on iron, invoking Ogun. He represents the power that comes from hard, honest work.

**Shango:** The orisha of thunder and lightning. Shango was said to have been a real king who became an orisha after his death. His justice is swift and sometimes frightening — like a lightning bolt. He represents the power that punishes wrongdoing instantly.

**Oshun:** The orisha of rivers, love, beauty, and fertility. Her offerings include honey and oranges. She represents the sweetness of life — desire, pleasure, abundance — but also the power and unpredictability of flowing water. She is one of the most widely loved orishas across Africa and the diaspora.

**Yemoja (Yemaya):** The orisha of the ocean and motherhood. She is the protector of children and the mother of many orishas. In the diaspora — in Brazil, Cuba, and the Caribbean — she became especially important to people who had crossed the ocean in slave ships. She is the mother who protects her children even in the most terrible circumstances.

### Part 4: The Ancestors Are Still Here

In many African traditions, the dead do not leave. They become ancestors — members of the family who have crossed from this world to the next but who remain present, watchful, and involved in the lives of the living.

This is not a vague idea about "remembering" the dead. It is a serious belief that the dead are real, active beings who can help, warn, and guide you — and who can be offended if you neglect them. Families set aside food and drink for their ancestors. They consult them before important decisions. They honor them at ceremonies and festivals.

Think about what this means for how you live: if your grandmother is still watching, still part of the family, you cannot simply forget her. You owe her respect and remembrance. Your actions reflect not just on you but on everyone who came before you. The family extends backward in time — the living and the dead together form one community.

### Part 5: Ubuntu — "I Am Because We Are"

One of the most powerful ideas to come from Africa is ubuntu. The word comes from the Bantu languages of Southern Africa, and it is often translated as: "A person is a person through other persons."

What does this mean? It means that you do not become fully human all by yourself. You become human through your relationships — with your family, your community, your ancestors. A person cut off from all others is not a free individual (as Western culture might say) — they are incomplete.

Ubuntu is not just a nice idea about being kind to people. It is a deep belief about what a human being actually is. In the ubuntu view, your identity — who you are as a person — is created through your connections with others. This is very different from the Western idea that each person is a separate individual first and enters into relationships second.

Nelson Mandela and Desmond Tutu both used ubuntu as the foundation for rebuilding South Africa after apartheid. When Mandela chose forgiveness over revenge, he was drawing on ubuntu — the belief that destroying your enemy destroys part of yourself, because your humanity is bound up with theirs.

### Part 6: The Sacred World — Rivers, Forests, and Places

In many African traditions, the natural world is not just a backdrop for human life. It is alive with spiritual power. A river is not just water — it may be the home of Oshun, and treating it with disrespect offends the divine. A forest is not just trees — it is a place where spirits dwell and where the boundary between the human world and the spirit world grows thin.

This way of seeing the world has something in common with the Celtic belief in sacred places and the Aboriginal Australian understanding of the Dreaming. In all three traditions, the land itself is holy — not because someone declared it holy, but because divinity lives there.

### Part 7: Anansi and the Trickster Tradition

Anansi the Spider is one of the most famous characters in West African storytelling — an Akan and Ashanti tradition. Anansi is small, clever, and always getting into trouble. He uses his wits to outsmart bigger, stronger creatures. Sometimes his tricks work beautifully. Sometimes they backfire.

Anansi is a trickster — a figure found in traditions around the world (Loki in Norse mythology, Coyote in Native American traditions, Eshu in Yoruba tradition). The trickster breaks rules, but in breaking them, he reveals something true. He shows that cleverness can overcome power. He shows that the rules are not as fixed as they seem. And sometimes he shows that breaking rules has consequences.

Anansi traveled to the Americas with enslaved Akan and Ashanti people. His stories survived the Middle Passage and are told today in the Caribbean, in the United States, and across the African diaspora. Anansi is a survivor — like the people who carried his stories.

### Part 8: Adinkra — Wisdom You Can See

The Akan people of Ghana created Adinkra symbols — small, beautiful designs, each one representing a concept, a proverb, or a piece of wisdom. These symbols are stamped on cloth, carved into buildings, and worn as jewelry.

For example:
- **Sankofa** — a bird looking backward while flying forward. It means: "Go back and get it." You must learn from the past to move into the future.
- **Gye Nyame** — meaning "except for God." It represents the supremacy of God over all things.
- **Dwennimmen** — the horns of a ram. It represents strength combined with humility.

Adinkra symbols are a way of carrying wisdom visually — a tradition of philosophy expressed through art rather than through written text.

### Part 9: What Survived the Middle Passage

Something extraordinary happened during the Atlantic slave trade. Enslaved African people — stripped of everything, forbidden to practice their religions, separated from their communities — carried their beliefs with them in their memories. They could not bring their sacred objects. They could not openly worship their gods. But they remembered.

In Brazil, enslaved Yoruba people preserved their tradition by connecting each orisha to a Catholic saint. Shango became Saint Barbara. Yemoja became Our Lady of the Sea. Ogun became Saint George. On the surface, they were praying to Catholic saints. Underneath, they were keeping their ancient traditions alive. This tradition became Candomble, which is still practiced by millions of people in Brazil today.

In Haiti, traditions from the Fon and Ewe peoples of Dahomey (modern Benin) combined with other African traditions and Catholic elements to create Vodou — a living religion that played a direct role in the Haitian Revolution, the only successful large-scale slave revolution in history.

This survival is one of the most remarkable achievements in all of religious history. Against the deliberate destruction of their culture, enslaved African people preserved their deepest beliefs and passed them to their children. That is not just religion. That is an act of spiritual resistance.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Orisha Stories [Ongoing]
Tell or read the stories of the major orishas — Eshu, Ogun, Shango, Oshun, Yemoja, Obatala. After each story, the student narrates: Who is this orisha? What part of life do they govern? What kind of person would pray to them?

### Study 2: The Ancestor Conversation [Early Packet]
Discuss the concept of ancestors as present members of the family. Ask: What would it change if you believed your grandmother was still watching? How would it change the way you behave? Is this idea strange, or is it similar to things you already believe?

### Study 3: Ubuntu Discussion [Mid-Packet]
Introduce ubuntu: "I am because we are." Discuss: What does this mean? Is it true that you need other people to be fully yourself? What would it be like to have no family, no friends, no community? How is this different from the idea that each person is completely independent?

### Study 4: Anansi Stories [Mid-Packet]
Read Anansi stories aloud. Compare Anansi to other trickster figures the student has encountered (Loki, Coyote, Hermes). What do tricksters have in common? Why do so many cultures have trickster stories?

### Study 5: Adinkra Art Project [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Study Adinkra symbols together. The student picks three favorites and explains what they mean. Then the student designs their own symbol representing a piece of wisdom they think is important.

### Study 6: The Survival Story [Late Packet]
Tell the story of how African traditions survived the Middle Passage. Discuss: How did people keep their beliefs alive when they were forbidden to practice them? What does it mean that Oshun is still worshipped in Brazil today?

### Study 7: Comparing Ideas About God [End of Packet]
The Yoruba believe in one supreme God (Olodumare) who works through many orishas. Christians believe in one God who may be approached through saints. How are these similar? How are they different? What does each approach tell you about what people believe about God?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Seriousness of Engagement
**Developing well:** The student discusses African traditions with the same respect they bring to other religions. They use specific names (Olodumare, Oshun, Shango) and can describe specific beliefs and practices.
**Developing slowly:** The student treats the material as entertaining but not serious. Generic references to "African gods" rather than specific engagement.
**Diagnostic:** Check your own presentation. If you are treating this as a unit to "get through," the student will absorb that attitude.

### 2. Understanding of Key Concepts
**Developing well:** The student can explain the orisha system, ancestor veneration, and ubuntu in their own words with reasonable accuracy.
**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish between these concepts or reduces everything to "they believe in nature spirits."

### 3. The Survival Narrative
**Developing well:** The student can narrate how African traditions survived the Middle Passage and explain why this is remarkable.
**Developing slowly:** The student has no sense of what the survival required or why it matters.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### The "Primitive Religion" Frame
If you or the student frame these traditions as earlier stages of religious development — as if they are what people believed "before" they got Christianity or Islam — you have fundamentally distorted the material. These are complete, sophisticated religious systems. Many are still practiced. They are not historical curiosities.

### Lumping Everything Together
"African religion" is not one thing. The Yoruba tradition is different from Zulu religion, which is different from Akan tradition. Use specific names and specific traditions. The student should come away knowing that Africa contains enormous religious diversity.

### Avoiding the Hard Parts
The survival story involves slavery. At Stretching Branches, the student does not need the full horror of the Middle Passage, but they need to understand that people were taken from their homes, forbidden to practice their religion, and that keeping their beliefs alive was an act of resistance against real oppression. Do not sanitize this into irrelevance.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: The Yoruba Idea of God
**Strong:** Describes Olodumare as the supreme God, explains why the orishas exist as intermediaries, and can name specific orishas.
**Weak:** "They believed in lots of gods." This misses the monotheistic structure of Yoruba theology.

### Prompt 3: Ubuntu
**Strong:** Explains the concept in their own words. May compare it to Western individualism or to their own experience.
**Weak:** "Be nice to people." This reduces a philosophical concept to a moral platitude.

### Prompt 5: Survival of Traditions
**Strong:** Narrates the specific mechanism — orishas associated with saints, secret practice — and expresses awareness of what this required.
**Weak:** "They kept believing." No specifics, no sense of the difficulty.
